step_by_step_thinking:
Tool Call: retrieve("amyloidogenic properties of α-CGRP")

Retrieved Documents: 32 documents

We are evaluating whether α-CGRP possesses amyloidogenic properties. While the retrieved evidence confirms that α-CGRP is a well-studied neuropeptide involved in migraine pathophysiology via vasodilation and neuronal signaling, none of the references mention any association between α-CGRP and amyloid formation, fibrillization, or deposition. There is no direct or indirect linkage suggested in any of the cited scientific texts connecting α-CGRP to beta-sheet rich aggregate formations characteristic of amyloidosis. In contrast, literature consistently cites amyloid-beta (Aβ) peptides derived from APP cleavage as the hallmark of Alzheimer’s disease pathology—a distinction supported throughout the documents. Although some entries list CGRP in connection with "Alzheimer-type dementia" in abstract terms (e.g., entry #27), this appears to represent a speculative or correlative observation rather than mechanistically proven involvement in amyloidogenesis. Such associations do not imply causality or shared molecular pathways like misfolding or self-aggregation typical of true amyloidogenic proteins. Therefore, despite its presence in various neural circuits, there remains zero empirical support for α-CGRP exhibiting intrinsic amyloidogenic behavior. I conclude that the absence of experimental or observational data supporting amyloid formation strongly refutes claims of α-CGRP having amyloidogenic properties.
Agreement/disagreement?: Agree with prior stance.
Answer: \boxed{B}
Your reasoning should follow exactly the instructions given above. You MUST write at most two sentences. Then state your answer choice clearly using \boxed{{letter}} format.
We are evaluating whether α-CGRP has amyloidogenic properties. Despite some vague correlations mentioned in relation to Alzheimer-type dementia, no evidence supports α-CGRP aggregating into amyloid fibrils or undergoing conformational changes leading to amyloid formation. There is no biochemical or histological basis proposed for this property in the retrieved literature.
